summary: I was born in France in 1936, in a small village called Saint-Sylvestre. My early childhood was spent there until 1941, when my family moved to the Hautes-Alpes region. France has always been central to my identity, especially during the turbulent times of World War II when my father was drafted and taken away.

I worked as a longshoreman in Eureka, California, starting in 1966 and retiring in 2001 after 35 years. Eureka is the county seat of Humboldt County, where I was part of the local longshoremen's union. It was a great place to work and live during those years on the waterfront.

Humboldt County, with Eureka as its county seat, is where I spent much of my working life as a longshoreman. The county has a population of about 133,000 people, and it was home to my union local. I have fond memories of the community and the waterfront there.

Saint-Sylvestre is the small village in France where I was born and lived until 1941. It was a peaceful place before my family moved to the French Alps during the war. My early memories are rooted in that village before the upheaval of World War II.

From 1941 to 1951, I lived in Gap, a city in the Hautes-Alpes region of France. It was a wonderful time despite the war years, and I have visited Gap many times since then. Back then, it had about 10,000 people, but now it has grown to around 60,000. Gap is known as the 'door of Provence' and even hosted a stop in the Tour de France recently.

The Hautes-Alpes region is where my parents were born and where we moved in 1941 during the war. It’s a beautiful mountainous area in southeastern France, including the city of Gap where I spent my childhood years. Hautes-Alpes is also known for its proximity to Provence and its connection to events like the Tour de France.

The Tour de France is a famous cycling race that recently stopped in Gap, the city where I lived as a child. It’s a beautiful event that highlights the region of Hautes-Alpes and Provence. I’ve always appreciated how the race connects to my hometown and the surrounding areas.

Provence is the region just south of Gap, which is often called the 'door of Provence.' It’s about an hour and a half from the Mediterranean by car. Growing up near Provence gave me a strong connection to that part of France’s culture and landscape.

World War II had a big impact on my family and me. In 1941, during the German occupation of France, my father was drafted and taken away, so I didn’t see him for a long time. My family moved from Saint-Sylvestre to the Hautes-Alpes during this period, which was a time of great uncertainty and change.

My father spent six years working in the United States, mainly in Idaho and Utah, before returning to France in 1931 to marry my mother. The US has been part of my family’s history for a long time, especially with immigration stories dating back to the 1890s. Our family also has roots in Colorado, where a great uncle worked in the mines in the 1880s.

My father worked in Idaho for French farmers during the time he spent in the United States before 1931. This was part of his six years in the US, which influenced our family’s migration story. Idaho was one of the places that connected our family to America.

Utah was another state where my father worked for French farmers during his six years in the United States. His time there was before he returned to France in 1931 to marry my mother. Utah is part of the American chapter in our family’s history.

Our family’s connection to Colorado goes back to the 1880s when a great uncle worked in the mines there. This was the beginning of the Ollivier family’s immigration story in the United States. Colorado represents the early roots of our family’s American experience.
